analogy: Think of "Judiciary" through the chapter's own opening images: 124 Indian Constitution at Work Chapter Six JUDICIARY INTRODUCTION Many times, courts are seen only as arbitrators in disputes between individuals or private parties. But judiciary performs some political functions also. Judiciary is an important organ of the government. The Supreme Court of India is... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: JUDICIARY, INTRODUCTION, WHY DO WE NEED AN INDEPENDENT JUDICIARY?, READ A CARTOON. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Judiciary is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 124 Indian Constitution at Work Chapter Six JUDICIARY INTRODUCTION Many times, courts are seen only as arbitrators in disputes between individuals or private parties.

The text explains that but judiciary performs some political functions also.

The text explains that judiciary is an important organ of the government.

The text explains that the Supreme Court of India is in fact, one of the very powerful courts in the world.

The text explains that right from 1950 the judiciary has played an important role in interpreting and in protecting the Constitution.

The text explains that in this chapter you will study the role and importance of the judiciary.

The text explains that in the chapter on fundamental rights you have already read that the judiciary is very important for protecting our rights.

The text explains that after studying this chapter, you would be able to understand ± the meaning of independence of judiciary; ± the role of Indian Judiciary in protecting our rights; ± the role of the Judiciary in interpreting the Constitution; and ± the relationship between the Judiciary and the Parliament of India.

The text explains that 125 Chapter 6: Judiciary WHY DO WE NEED AN INDEPENDENT JUDICIARY?

The text explains that in any society, disputes are bound to arise between individuals, between groups and between individuals or groups and government.

The text explains that all such disputes must be settled by an independent body in accordance with the principle of rule of law.

The text explains that this idea of rule of law implies that all individuals — rich and poor, men or women, forward or backward castes — are subjected to the same law.

The text explains that the principal role of the judiciary is to protect rule of law and ensure supremacy of law.

The text explains that it safeguards rights of the individual, settles disputes in accordance with the law and ensures that democracy does not give way to individual or group dictatorship.

The text explains that in order to be able to do all this, it is necessary that the judiciary is independent of any political pressures.

The text explains that independence of Judiciary Simply stated independence of judiciary means that ± the other organs of the government like the executive and legislature must not restrain the functioning of the judiciary in such a way that it is unable to do justice. ± the other organs of the government should not interfere with the decision of the judiciary. ± judges must be able to perform their functions without fear or favour.

The text explains that judiciary is a part of the democratic political structure of the No fisticuffs please, this is rule of law!

The text explains that it is therefore accountable to the Constitution, to the democratic traditions and to the people of the country.

The text explains that the Indian Constitution has ensured the independence of the judiciary through a number of measures.

The text explains that the legislature is not involved in the process of appointment of judges.

The text explains that thus, it was believed that party politics would not play a role in the process of appointments.

The text explains that the Constitution prescribes a very difficult procedure for removal of judges.

The text explains that the Constitution makers believed that a difficult procedure of removal would provide security of office to the members of judiciary.

The text explains that the Constitution provides that the salaries and allowances of the judges are not subjected to the approval of the legislature.

The text explains that i remember the case of Machal mentioned in chapter two.

The text explains that 127 Chapter 6: Judiciary Activity Hold a debate in class on the following topic.

The text explains that do you think these are justified? ± Constitution ± Precedents ± Opinion of other courts ± Public opinion ± Media ± Traditions of law ± Laws ± Time and staff constraints ± Fear of public criticism ± Fear of action by executive Appointment of Judges The appointment of judges has never been free from political controversy.

The text explains that it makes a difference who serves in the Supreme Court and High Court— a difference in how the Constitution is interpreted.

The text explains that council of Ministers, Governors and Chief Ministers and Chief Justice of India — all influence the process of judicial appointment.

The text explains that 128 Indian Constitution at Work The other Judges of the Supreme Court and the High Court are appointed by the President after ‘consulting’ the CJI.

The text explains that we have studied this in the chapter on Elections.

The text explains that 129 Chapter 6: Judiciary both balance of power and independence of the judiciary.

The text explains that 130 Indian Constitution at Work STRUCTURE OF THE JUDICIARY The Constitution of India provides for a single integrated judicial system.

The text explains that the structure of the judiciary in India is pyramidal with the Supreme Court at the top, High Courts below them and district and subordinate courts at the lowest level (see the diagram below).

The text explains that the lower courts function under the direct superintendence of the higher courts.

The text explains that high Court ± Can hear appeals from lower courts. ± Can issue writs for restoring Fundamental Rights. ± Can deal with cases within the jurisdiction of the State. ± Exercises superintendence and control over courts below it.

The text explains that 131 Chapter 6: Judiciary Jurisdiction of Supreme Court The Supreme Court of India is one of the very powerful courts anywhere in the world.

The text explains that however, it functions within the limitations imposed by the Constitution.

The text explains that the functions and responsibilities of the Supreme Court are defined by the Constitution.

The text explains that appellate Tries appeals from lower courts in Civil, Criminal and Constitutional cases Advisory Advises the President on matters of public importance and law Writ: Can issue writs of Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Prohibition, Certiorari and Quo warranto to protect the Fundamental Rights of the individual.

The text explains that 132 Indian Constitution at Work Original Jurisdiction Original jurisdiction means cases that can be directly considered by the Supreme Court without going to the lower courts before that.

The text explains that what similarities or differences do you find between this example and the judicial system in India? “It would be helpful if a body of common law, or judicial precedent, existed that could clarify a journalist’s rights.

The text explains that unfortunately, Ecuador’s courts don’t work that way.

The text explains that judges are not forced to respect the rulings of higher courts in previous cases.

The text explains that unlike the US, an appellate judge in Ecuador (or elsewhere in South America, for that matter) need not provide a written decision explaining the legal basis of a ruling.

The text explains that a judge may rule one way today and the opposite way, in a similar case, tomorrow, without explaining why.”

The text explains that read the following statements: Match them with the different jurisdictions the Supreme Court can exercise - Original, Appellate, and Advisory. √ The government wanted to know if it can pass a law about the citizenship status of residents of Pakistan-occupied areas of Jammu and Kashmir. √ In order to resolve the dispute about river Cauvery the government of Tamil Nadu wants to approach the court. √ Court rejected the appeal by people against the eviction from the dam site.

The text explains that in what way can public interest litigation help the poor?

The text explains that do you think that judicial activism can lead to a conflict between the judiciary and the executive?

The text explains that how is judicial activism related to the protection of fundamental rights?

The text explains that has it helped in expanding the scope of fundamental rights?

Source: NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work, Chapter 6: Judiciary
